title: SEGMENT 12 — Haploidentical transplant: the access revolution

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Haploidentical transplant: the access revolution
For decades, the central access problem in transplant was HLA matching.
If you didn't have a matched sibling (25 percent chance), you went to the registry, where most non-European patients had limited or no options.
Cord blood helped but not enough.
Then, in the early 2000s, a group at Johns Hopkins led by Ephraim Fuchs, Leo Luznik, and Rick Jones developed a regimen that changed the calculus completely.
It's called the Baltimore platform for haploidentical transplant, and it has opened HSCT to essentially any patient with a living first-degree relative.
A haploidentical donor is half-matched — sharing one HLA haplotype with the recipient.
By definition, every parent of a patient is haploidentical, as are all children, and 50 percent of siblings.
So every patient with a living parent, child, or non-matched sibling has a haploidentical donor.
The access problem, in principle, disappears.
The historic obstacle was immunology.
Half-matched transplants were associated with catastrophic rates of GVHD and graft rejection — the immunological wars in both directions were brutal.
Multiple groups in the 1980s and 1990s tried elaborate T-cell depletion strategies with limited success.
The Baltimore breakthrough was elegantly simple.
After infusion of the unmanipulated half-matched graft, on days +3 and +4 post-transplant, the patient is given a single high dose of cyclophosphamide.

Cyclophosphamide preferentially kills proliferating T cells — and the most rapidly proliferating T cells at that moment are the alloreactive ones that have just woken up and started dividing in response to recipient antigen.
Selective elimination of the alloreactive subset, in vivo, post-transplant.
The non-alloreactive T cells, dividing more slowly, survive.
Regulatory T cells, which are particularly resistant to cyclophosphamide, are spared and help establish tolerance.
The result is a haploidentical transplant with rates of severe GVHD comparable to matched unrelated donor transplants.
The procedure has been refined over twenty years, has been adopted globally, and has transformed access to allogeneic HSCT.
Patients of any ancestry who have any first-degree relative can now be transplanted.
The numbers are striking.
In 2005, haploidentical transplants were a tiny fraction of allogeneic procedures globally.
By 2020, they were one of the fastest-growing categories, particularly in Asia, Latin America, and among minority populations in the US and Europe.
The Baltimore protocol — post-transplant cyclophosphamide, or PTCy — is now also being used with matched and mismatched unrelated donors to reduce GVHD across the board.
It is one of the most important clinical innovations in transplant medicine of the last twenty years.
This is what I mean by an access revolution.
A procedure that was, for fifty years, structurally limited to patients with matched donors became — through a clever bit of immunology — available to almost everyone.
Equity through biology.
